front.title: Base YANG Model for any Service

abstract.t: This document defines a modular service modeling stack that extracts technology-agnostic constructs into a base ietf-svc module, separates Ethernet-specific treatment into ietf-eth-svc, and layers VPN-oriented intent in ietf-vpn-svc, with bis versions of L2VPN and L3VPN services augmenting the shared foundation.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: The L2SM and L3SM YANG models provide a standardized approach to modeling L2VPN and L3VPN services. However, these models do not interact with each other, and they are not designed to be extended to model other types of services. This document proposes a base YANG model for any service, which can be used as a starting point for modeling specific customer services. The goals of this work are to reduce duplication between existing L2SM and L3SM work, enable new service types to reuse common abstractions, and let operators expose a consistent customer-facing interface while retaining flexibility for technology-specific extensions.

section.name: Limitations of the current Service Models
Limited Interoperability and Extensibility
section.t:
The YANG Service Models in (L3SM) and (L2SM) capture Layer 3 and Layer 2 VPN service intent at an abstraction level that is directly applicable to the agreements between an operator and their customers.
These models individually cover all the inputs to deliver either L2VPN or L3VPN services. However, a provider who wants to offer both types of services must manage two separate models and/or request their customers to enter a lot of duplicate information. It is difficult to express certain relationships between the L2VPN and L3VPN services being ordered, such as diversity or bandwidth constraints.
More broadly, operators and their customers would benefit from a common service modeling framework that can be reused across different service types, and that can capture the relationships and constraints.
Examples of services that could be modeled on top of the same base include the already standardized L2VPN and L3VPN services, but also non-VPN services such as (business) internet access, cloud connectivity, or even optical wavelength services. The common service model would allow operators to maintain a consistent customer-facing interface across different service types, and to more easily introduce new service types in the future.

section.name: Missing support for network layering
section.t: The current L2SM and L3SM models do not have a way to express the relationships between different service layers. For example, an operator may want to specify that an L3VPN service should be delivered over a specific L2VPN service, or that an SD-WAN service should be delivered over a specific internet access service or MPLS-based L3VPN. This lack of support for network layering makes it difficult to model and manage complex service offerings that involve multiple layers of abstraction and different technologies.

section.name: Over-abstraction hinders clear agreement
section.t: The L3SM abstracts away from the underlying network technologies and focuses on the service intent. While this is a strength in terms of technology-agnostic modeling, it can also make it difficult to capture certain service attributes that are relevant to the customer or to the delivery of the service.

For example, the L3SM has no method of specifying a port and/or VLAN on a Site Device on which a Site Network Access should be delivered. This can lead to ambiguity in the service specification, and it can make it difficult for operators to ensure that the service is delivered according to the customer's intent.

The constraints mechanism goes through great lengths to avoid identifying the specific (number of) bearers that should be used to deliver a service. The ordering process for many operators today is much more explicit in naming the bearers, along with an explicit mapping of each Site Network Access to those bearers. At least giving the purchaser the option of providing their requirements in such an explicit manner would guarantee that the operator can deliver a service according to the customer's actual requirements.

section.name: Proposed solution
section.t: This document revisits the L3SM and L2SM models and introduces a family of YANG modules that factor out the common service concepts into a shared base. The new ietf-svc module provides a technology-agnostic service skeleton covering sites, site-network- accesses, diversity constraints, and bearer hints. Ethernet-centric constructs such as bandwidth profiles and QoS classification are moved into a dedicated ietf-eth-svc module so that Ethernet- based services can share the same treatment primitives without cluttering the core service skeleton.

The ietf-vpn-svc module builds on that base to capture VPN-oriented attributes such as service type, topology, cloud or extranet reachability, carriers' carrier, and policy filters. Bis versions of ietf-l3vpn-svc and ietf-l2vpn-svc then augment the shared service framework with technology-specific parameters. The L3VPN module retains the routing, multicast, address-allocation, NAT, and BFD constructs from . The L2VPN module retains the Ethernet- and OAM-specific behaviors from (e.g., bundling, CE-VLAN preservation, L2CP handling, CFM, and Y.1731) while leveraging the shared service skeleton, the Ethernet treatment constructs in ietf-eth-svc, and the VPN service identities from ietf-vpn-svc.

The augment relationships across the modules are shown in ; the path is linear, with ietf-svc at the root, ietf-eth-svc augmenting the base skeleton, and ietf-vpn-svc augmenting both ietf-eth-svc and ietf-svc before the VPN-specific bis modules attach.

figure.name: Service module augmentation relationships
figure.artwork: module: ietf-svc (Base service skeleton) +-- augment: ietf-eth-svc (Ethernet attributes, e.g QoS) +-- augment: ietf-vpn-svc (VPN service common attributes) +-- augment: ietf-l2vpn-svc (L2VPN-specific attributes) +-- augment: ietf-l3vpn-svc (L3VPN-specific attributes)

section.t: By aligning the L2SM and L3SM models on a common foundation, this work reduces duplication, improves consistency of service exposure to customers and OSS/BSS systems, and simplifies the introduction of future service types that can reuse the same base abstractions.

section.name: Relationships to other work
section.t: The network layer models, iet-l3vpn-ntw and iet-l2vpn-ntw may benefit from a similar modularization approach, but they are outside the scope of this document.
Adoption of the common groupings defined in ietf-vpn-common at the service model level may be beneficial and should be considered as part of this work.

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This memo includes no request to IANA.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: The security posture of this document follows the guidance in the L3SM . The NETCONF Access Control Model (NACM) should be used to restrict protocol operations and data nodes to authorized principals.

New augments added to the base service skeleton must describe any additional security-sensitive leaves and specify suitable NACM rules. When encryption or authentication material is conveyed (e.g., keys or credentials), operators must ensure secure transport and operational processes (rotation, revocation, audit) consistent with their policy.
